_meta.note: Prototype data for Troop 79 Advancement system. Maya Sankpal-Tatera's record is taken from her real Clipboard PDF; other scouts are illustrative.
_meta.source: Clipboard for - Maya Sankpal-Tatera.pdf + Troop 79 Advancement Spreadsheet

requirements.label: Sharpen & care of knife ax saw
Use Edge to teach Square Knot
Select a patrol site/pitch a tent
Explain when use fire for cooking
Budget, plan to earn an item
Ten More Merit Badges (6 Eagle-required, 21 total)

meritBadgeRequirements._note: Illustrative requirement lists for prototype testing. Wording is paraphrased — replace with exact text from the current official BSA pamphlet before going live, and re-check on every BSA annual update. The 'complete' field encodes optionality: 'all' (must complete every child), 'any' (any one child counts), or 'n-of' with completeN.

citizenship-world.label: Explain what citizenship in the world means to you and what makes a good world citizen
Explain U.S. citizenship rights/duties/obligations; compare with two other countries

children.label: Discuss how national interest and foreign relationships affect a country's security, economy, values, and health (current event)
Discuss how geography, resources, and climate shape a foreign country's economy and global partnerships

citizenship-world.label: International law, trade, and organizations (do TWO)

children.label: Explain international law vs. national law and its role in conflict resolution
Research a current international trade/finance issue; explain why countries must cooperate
Select TWO organizations (UN/UNICEF, ICJ, Interpol, WOSM, WHO, Amnesty Intl, IFRC, CARE, EU) and describe their role
Discuss differences between constitutional and nonconstitutional governments
Name at least five government types currently in power in the world
Show on a world map countries using each of those five government types
Explain how governments are represented abroad and how the U.S. is accredited to international organizations
Describe the roles of ambassador, consul, Bureau of Global Public Affairs, and the U.S. Commercial Service
Explain the purpose of a passport and visa for international travel
Explore an issue on the U.S. State Department website
Find a human rights story recognized in the U.S. but not in another country (foreign news source)
Visit with a student or Scout from another country; discuss values, holidays, foods, and traditions
Attend or participate in a WOSM event (Jamboree, Moot, Congress, JOTA/JOTI) or a national/regional Jamboree
Attend or participate in an international event in your area (festival, concert, play)

citizenship-nation.label: Explain what the Constitution is, what it does, its principles, and why it matters
List the Constitution's six purposes from the Preamble and how they affect your family and community

children.label: Explain the function of each branch
Explain why power is divided among the branches
Explain how each branch checks and balances the others
Explain how citizens can be involved in each branch

citizenship-nation.label: Declaration, Bill of Rights, and national motto

children.label: Discuss the importance of the Declaration of Independence
Discuss the importance of the Bill of Rights and the 14th Amendment
Discuss the importance of E Pluribus Unum

citizenship-nation.label: Follow national news for five days; discuss issues and explain how one affects you, family, and community

children.label: Identify the speech's author
Explain the historical context
Explain the difficulties the nation faced that the speech addressed
Summarize what the author said
Explain why the speech is important to the nation's history
Choose a meaningful sentence from the speech and explain why

citizenship-nation.label: National sites and civic institutions (do TWO)

children.label: Visit a National Historic Landmark or Register site and discuss what you learned
Tour your state capitol or the U.S. Capitol and discuss its function and history
Tour a federal facility and explain its function in the community
Research a U.S. national monument and explain its importance

citizenship-nation.label: Name your Congressional representatives; write to one about a national issue and show the correspondence and any response

children.label: Discuss food allergies, intolerances, and dietary/religious needs
Explain food spoilage and safe storage at home and outdoors
Describe foodborne illness causes and prevention
Demonstrate handwashing and dishwashing technique
Show first-aid for common cooking injuries (cuts, burns, choking)
Plan a balanced day of meals
Identify each food group and its role
Describe portion sizes for your age
Plan a sample day of three meals plus one snack
Discuss hydration in different conditions
Discuss how to adjust a menu for special diets
Describe roles of each food group
Identify potential issues with overweight/underweight
Define common cooking terms (sauté, simmer, etc.)
Explain dry vs liquid measurements
Read and interpret a recipe and a nutrition label
Identify common kitchen tools and their safe use
Plan a menu of breakfast, lunch, dinner, and one snack/dessert
Make a shopping list within a budget
Prepare each meal with adult supervision
Time the preparation and discuss what went well
Clean up after each meal safely
Plan a camp menu for two days
Prepare a meal over a camp stove
Prepare a meal over a fire
Demonstrate safe food storage in camp
Plan a one-day trail menu suitable for backpacking
Prepare a hot meal with a lightweight backpacking stove
Discuss water purification on the trail

citizenship-community.label: Discuss what citizenship in the community means and how to be a good citizen

children.label: Map community landmarks (government, fire/police/hospital, parks, historic); record distances and travel times
Chart your local or state government; show top offices and elected vs. appointed
Attend an in-person council, school board, court session, or approved government meeting
Explain why you agree with one opinion over another on an issue from the meeting
Find which branch of local government is responsible for an issue important to your community
Interview someone from that branch about the issue and how young people can help
Share what you learned with your counselor

citizenship-community.label: Watch an approved movie about individuals positively affecting a community; discuss
List taxpayer-funded community services and why they matter to your community

children.label: Identify three charitable organizations (outside Scouting) that bring your community together
Research one of the organizations using a variety of resources
Contact the organization and volunteer at least eight hours; discuss what you learned

citizenship-community.label: Develop and stage a public presentation on your community's history, cultures, features, and challenges

children.label: Explain how to assess and handle a first aid emergency, including scene safety
Tell how to get emergency medical help from home and from a remote wilderness location
Demonstrate precautions against infection, including safe disposal of used supplies
Demonstrate evaluating and managing a patient's airway and breathing
Demonstrate a thorough examination of an accident victim
Discuss why shock is an emergency
Define triage and describe example triage situations
Obtain a Scout Annual Health and Medical Record and discuss its importance for first aid
Assemble a hiking first-aid kit and demo each item; inspect a unit/home/vehicle/camp kit

first-aid.label: Wounds with no external bleeding (signs, first aid, prevention)

children.label: Closed wounds (bruise, hematoma)
Thermal burns or scalds (superficial, partial, and full thickness)
Chemical burns
Electrical burns
Sunburn
Snow blindness
Immersion foot, frostnip, frostbite, and ice burns
Abrasions such as chafing and rope burns
Blisters on hands, feet, buttocks, and shoulders
Puncture wounds from splinters, nails, and fish hooks
Rash from poisonous plants
Bug bites (chiggers, ticks, mosquitoes, biting gnats)
Bee stings
Spider bites
Scorpion sting
Bite of a pet, wild mammal, or human
Bite of a venomous snake
Nosebleed
Open wound with mild or moderate bleeding (scratch, scrape, shallow cut)
Open wound with severe bleeding, such as a deep cut on an arm or leg
When to use (and not use) a tourniquet; demonstrate application without tightening
Choking
Asthmatic attack
Anaphylaxis from insect sting or food/product allergy
Inhalation injuries
Altitude sickness

first-aid.label: Loss of consciousness (signs, first aid, prevention)

children.label: Fainting
Hypoglycemia
Seizure
Drug overdose and alcohol poisoning
Underwater hypoxic blackout
Cold water shock and drowning
Lightning strike and electric shock
Explain what a heart attack is
Describe heart attack symptoms, signs, and first aid
Describe the conditions required before performing CPR
Demonstrate proper CPR technique on an approved training device
Explain the use of an AED
Demonstrate or simulate proper AED use
Identify AED locations at public facilities; discuss why those locations
Explain differences among strains, tears, ruptures, sprains, dislocations, and fractures
Signs and first aid for muscle strain, muscle tear, and tendon rupture
Signs and complications of a sprain, fracture, and dislocation
Demonstrate bandages: arm sling; elastic/cravat wraps for ankle sprain and wrist/hand
Demonstrate splinting: finger/toe, forearm/wrist, upper leg, lower leg/ankle
Signs, complications, and prevention of head, neck, and back injuries
Signs and first aid for a concussion
Demonstrate first aid for an open head wound with a triangular or other bandage
Demonstrate first aid for a suspected neck or back injury
Describe when an injured person should and should not be moved
Tell how to choose the best method to move a person; demonstrate the methods
Alone and with a partner, transport a person from a smoke-filled room
Alone and with a partner, transport a person with a sprained ankle at least 25 yards
With helpers, improvise a stretcher and move an unconscious person at least 25 yards

first-aid.label: Heat- and cold-related conditions (signs, first aid, prevention)

children.label: Dehydration and over-hydration
Heat cramps and muscle pain after exertion
Heat exhaustion
Heat stroke
Chest pains associated with cold exposure
Hypothermia
Describe reactions to at least three stressful situations
Describe actions to prepare for and manage stressful situations
Describe indications someone might be a danger to themselves or others
Describe actions to take if you suspect someone is a danger to themselves or others
Object in the eye
Broken, chipped, loosened, or knocked-out tooth
Vomiting and diarrhea from food poisoning
Abdominal pain
Stroke

first-aid.label: Plan and teach a first-aid skill using the EDGE method

children.label: Visit an emergency medical station or training center; discuss what you learned
Interview an EMS professional about their work and career
Research three EMS careers, one in depth (training, costs, prospects)
Explore using EMS skills for a hobby or healthy lifestyle

environmental-science.label: Explain environmental science in your own words and how science helps understand, conserve, and improve it

children.label: Observe an approved area's ecosystem (sight, sound, smell) over two days
Note living, nonliving, and formerly-living ecosystem components, interactions, and human impacts
Explain PM sources and effects; test for particulates via an experiment
Discuss air pollution/transportation links (3 examples); compare two transportation modes
Research the Clean Air Act: history, goals, progress, impact, and enforcement
Identify community water source, treatment, and disposal; review a water quality report
Research a local flood- or drought-prone area's history and environmental impacts
Research the Clean Water Act: history, goals, progress, impact, and enforcement
List pesticides/fertilizers used in an area; discuss benefits and runoff/groundwater impacts
Explain erosion using a local example; discuss where sediment goes and how to reduce it
Research a Superfund site's cause, environmental effects, remediation, and current condition

environmental-science.label: Rare, Threatened, or Endangered Species (do ONE)

children.label: Research a state endangered species; present a 100-word report with a drawing or photo
Research a recovered species; present a 100-word report with a drawing or photo
Visit a habitat-improvement project for a threatened species; report to your troop

environmental-science.label: Pollution Prevention, Resource Recovery, and Conservation (do ONE)

children.label: List five resource-conservation methods; practice at least two for one week
Explain resource recovery; collect 10 Reduce/Reuse/Recycle examples and discuss handling
Identify five household hazardous-waste items; explain proper storage and disposal
List five regional pollinators/plants; explain their importance and how Scouts can help
Observe pollination for an hour; record pollinator-plant pairs and how Scouts can help
Explain pollination's importance to agriculture; identify four crop-pollinator pairs

environmental-science.label: Research two invasive species: origin, spread, ecosystem impact, and control methods
Identify environmental-impact topics for a construction project and evaluate alternatives
Identify three environmental-science careers; research one's training and salary prospects

children.label: Keep a one-day communication log; discuss what it reveals and how to improve
Keep a three-day listening journal covering inform, persuade, enjoy, and empathize
Observe a small group sharing personal stories; report on communication differences
List communication methods; discuss when each might not be appropriate or effective
Create and present a self-description (collage, story, drawing, song, or skit)
Build a sales plan; persuade your counselor to buy/use/agree; discuss effectiveness

communication.label: Write and deliver a five-minute speech at a group meeting
Interview someone; prepare a guest-speaker introduction and show how you'd invite them
Attend a public meeting with multiple viewpoints; take notes and write an objective report
Plan and teach a skill with teaching aids; assess whether the person learned it

children.label: Write a letter to the editor of a magazine or newspaper expressing your opinion
Create a webpage/blog with 3+ entries, a photo/illustration, and a link to another page
Desktop-publish a newsletter/brochure/flyer with an article and a photo or illustration

communication.label: Plan a court of honor, campfire, or worship service; get PLC approval and serve as MC
Identify three communication careers; research one's education/training/experience needs

aviation.label: Aviation basics and mechanics of flight

children.label: Define "aircraft"; describe three kinds of aircraft and their typical uses
Overview the evolution of flight; discuss three notable historical milestones
Explain fixed-wing vs. rotary-wing aircraft and the benefits of each
Explain how piston, turbine, and jet engines operate
Using a model aircraft, describe the four forces acting on it in flight
Explain how an airfoil generates lift, noting Bernoulli's principle
Identify an aircraft's control surfaces and explain how they control attitude/direction
Explain the purposes of a single-engine aircraft's flight instruments (attitude, altimeter, airspeed, etc.)
Build a model FPG-9 glider and compete on flight/landing precision with your troop
Build a rubber-band balsa airplane; fly it 25 feet straight with a smooth landing
Build a powered model aircraft/drone, get TRUST certified, and fly a successful takeoff/landing
Set and fly headings in a flight simulator with a successful takeoff and landing
Perform a supervised preflight inspection of an aircraft
Observe an aircraft maintenance activity; describe its maintenance schedule and requirements
Read an aeronautical chart; correct a measured course for variation, deviation, and wind drift
Take a discovery flight; record details and report your impressions
Visit an airport; report on facility use, runway numbering, and active-runway determination
Visit an FAA facility (ATCT, TRACON, ARTCC, or FSDO); report on its operation
Visit a military aviation facility; report on how it supports defense/civilian activities
Visit an aviation museum or air show; report on your impressions and takeaways
Explain the student, recreational, remote, and private pilot certificates
Describe the benefits of the instrument rating
Explain the commercial pilot certificate, ATP certificate, and CFI credential
Identify a local Aviation Exploring Post or Civil Air Patrol unit; learn its activities/requirements
Identify three aviation careers; research one in depth (training, prospects, salary)
Describe a physically fit person
Describe a mentally, emotionally, and socially fit person
Describe a spiritually fit person
Explain why it's important to be fit in all these ways
Discuss how personal fitness relates to the Scout Oath and Law
Discuss the importance of an annual physical exam and what it covers; note your last exam
Explain the importance of dental exams and daily oral care; note your last dental exam
Explain your exercise routine, whether it covers all four fitness components, and disease-risk impact
Discuss harmful substances you avoid and how that affects your risk factors
Explain vaccine-preventable diseases and your own immunization status
Discuss how nutrition relates to fitness and whether you eat a balanced diet
Discuss a healthy weight for you and how you maintain it
Explain the importance of adequate sleep and whether you get enough
Discuss quality family/social time and how you contribute to home life
Define a cardiorespiratory measure: timed 1-mile walk/run (or counselor-approved alternate)
Define two strength/endurance measures: sit-ups/plank, and push-ups/pull-ups (60 sec each)
Define a flexibility measure (e.g., sit-and-reach or back-scratch test)
Complete and record the baseline assessments defined in requirement 4
Identify your strongest/weakest fitness areas; choose one to target for improvement
Keep a three-day log of what you eat and drink
Set at least two diet/nutrition improvement goals based on your food log
Include a warm-up: low-intensity movement or stretching before each workout
Include a cardiorespiratory element: 15-30 minutes, at least three times per week
Include a muscular strength/endurance element targeting muscles per your improvement goals
Include a flexibility element (back stretches, trunk rotation, hamstring stretches)
Include a cool-down: low-intensity movement or stretching after each workout
Include a plan for achieving your two diet/nutrition improvement goals
Complete and log 12 consecutive weeks of the program (may resume after gaps under two weeks)
Repeat fitness assessments at weeks 4, 8, and after completion; show improvement over baseline
Keep 3-day food logs during weeks 8 and 12; show improvement toward your diet goals
Discuss your results, improvements, and insights with your counselor after completing the program
Research three personal-fitness careers; research one in depth (training, outlook, salary)
Research how a fitness area could support a hobby or lifestyle; research education/costs/organizations

personal-management.label: Family purchase, savings plan, and shopping strategy

children.label: Choose a major-expense item your family might want to purchase
Write a family savings plan for the item; discuss it with your counselor, family, and other family needs
Develop a shopping strategy: determine quality and comparison-shop at least two price sources
Prepare a 13-week budget of expected income, expenses, and savings
Compare expected income vs. expenses; plan to balance a deficit or use a surplus
Track actual income, expenses, and savings for the same 13 weeks; present results to your counselor
Compare budget to actual results; discuss with your counselor what you'd do differently
Emotions you feel when you receive money
How the amount of money you carry affects your spending habits
Buyer's remorse: your thoughts on a purchase right away vs. three months later
How hunger affects your food shopping
An item you bought after seeing ads for it; did it work as advertised?
What happens when you put money into a savings account
Charitable giving: its purpose and your thoughts on it
What you can do to better manage your money
Differences between saving and investing, and reasons for choosing one
Return on investment and risk, and how they're related
Simple interest vs. compound interest
Diversification in investing
Why it's important to save and invest for retirement
Common stocks
Mutual funds
Life insurance
A certificate of deposit (CD)
A savings account
A U.S. savings bond
Automobile insurance
Health insurance
Homeowner's/renter's insurance
Whole life and term life insurance
What a loan and interest are, and how APR measures a loan's true cost
Different ways to borrow money
Charge vs. debit vs. credit cards: costs/pitfalls and why minimum payments are unwise
Credit reports and how personal responsibility affects yours
Ways to reduce or eliminate debt
Write a to-do list for the coming week, ranked by importance
Make a seven-day schedule with set activities and planned times for your to-do tasks
Follow your one-week schedule; journal when tasks were actually completed vs. planned
Review your list/schedule/diary with your counselor; discuss what you'd do differently
Define the project and its goal
Develop a project timeline from start to completion
Describe your project
List needed resources and how each helps achieve your goal
Develop a budget for your project
Choose a post-graduation career; discuss its qualifications, education, skills, and experience
Explain the career's costs (tuition, supplies, room/board) and how you'd cover them

camping.label: Camping hazards, weather, and health and safety

children.label: Explain likely camping hazards and how to anticipate, prevent, mitigate, and respond to them
Discuss the importance of weather awareness and how to prepare if weather turns bad
Show first aid and prevention for common camp injuries/illnesses (hypothermia, frostbite, heat, dehydration, altitude sickness, insect/tick/snake bites, blisters, hyperventilation)

camping.label: Learn the Leave No Trace principles and Outdoor Code; write a personal/group plan to apply them on your next outing
Make a written overnight trek plan; show your route on a topo map using a compass, GPS receiver, or GPS app

children.label: Make a duty roster with assignments for an actual overnight campout
Help a patrol or Webelos unit prepare for an actual campout (roster, menu, equipment, planning, camp setup)
Prepare a warm- and cold-weather clothing list for overnight campouts; explain layering
Discuss footwear choices for different weather and why the right footwear matters
Explain proper care and storage of camping equipment (clothing, footwear, bedding)
List the Scout Basic Essentials for any campout and explain why each is needed
Present yourself and your pack to your Scoutmaster for inspection before an overnight campout

camping.label: Tents, campsites, packs, and sleeping bags

children.label: Describe four tent types and their care; pitch a tent with another Scout
Discuss camp sanitation and why water treatment matters; demonstrate two water treatment methods
Describe factors for choosing where to pitch a tent
Compare internal- and external-frame packs
Discuss sleeping bag types, care, and keeping it dry; make a comfortable ground bed

camping.label: Prepare for an overnight patrol campout

children.label: Make a checklist of personal and patrol gear needed
Pack your gear and share of patrol equipment/food for comfort, weight, balance, and quick access
Explain safety procedures for propane and liquid fuel stoves and proper fuel storage
Discuss advantages and disadvantages of different lightweight cooking stoves
Prepare a camp menu with recipes and a food list; plan two breakfasts, three lunches, two suppers; discuss food protection
While camping, cook a breakfast, lunch, and dinner from your planned menu; at least one must be a trail meal on a lightweight stove
Camp a total of at least 20 nights at designated Scouting activities/events
Do TWO: mountain hike (1,000+ ft gain), backpack/snowshoe/ski 4+ miles, bike 15+ miles, water trip 4+ hrs/5+ miles, snow camping, or a 30+ ft rappel
Perform a conservation project approved by the landowner or land-managing agency

camping.label: Discuss what earning this badge taught you about health, safety, survival, public health, conservation, and citizenship, and how Scout spirit/Oath/Law apply to camping

children.label: Explain likely woodworking hazards and how to prevent/mitigate/respond; explain tool safety precautions
Show first aid for woodworking injuries; explain eye/hearing protection and dust mask use
Earn the Totin' Chip
Describe how timber is grown, harvested, milled, and how lumber is cured, seasoned, graded, sized
Collect and label six kinds of wood; describe qualities and best uses of each
Show proper care, use, and storage of your tools and equipment
Sharpen the cutting edges of two different tools

woodwork.label: Build something useful with a saw, plane, hammer, brace, and bit, from lumber squared/measured to a working drawing
Design and build your own project; track time spent and material cost

children.label: Build a project with beveled or rounded edges from your own working drawings
Build a project with curved or incised cuttings from your own working drawings
Build a project with miter, dowel, or mortise-and-tenon joints from your own working drawings
Build a cabinet, box, or lidded item with inset hinges
Help make wooden toys for underprivileged children, or an approved woodworking service project for a charity

woodwork.label: Talk with a cabinetmaker or finish carpenter about training, careers, work conditions, pay, and unions

Use Edge to teach Square Knot
